shot.subject: web
shot.notes: dawn: a finished web strung with dew, every strand jeweled and lethal

segments.text: Every meal in this garden is sunlight, wearing a disguise. The light falls on the grass; the grass builds itself from it; the grazers eat the grass; and the hunters eat the grazers. Follow any mouthful back far enough and you arrive at the sun. This hour, we follow it forward — to the place where the chain pulls tight.

shot.subject: tree
shot.notes: the corridor between two great trunks, webs spanning it like rigged sails

segments.text: A corridor of air. A net across it. And on every side, citizens who must fly through.

shot.subject: spider
shot.notes: the builder at her hub, still as punctuation

segments.text: This is the Web of Danger.

shot.subject: web
shot.notes: glide slowly along the web's plane, the meadow refracted behind the silk

segments.text: The web is not a trap set in a wilderness. It is a filter placed across a flow of energy. The fireflies' night traffic runs through here; the ladybugs' commuting lanes run through here — and each carries a body's worth of the grass's stored sunlight, drawn toward a builder who cannot make her own. She is a predator, and a predator is simply the sun's energy taking a second passenger.

shot.subject: spider
shot.notes: macro on the forelegs resting against the hub's strands, reading

segments.text: Her tool is one of the finest materials in the living world. Spider silk is spun protein, drawn from her body as a liquid that hardens in the air — and for its weight, it rivals steel for strength. There are tens of thousands of spider species, and all of them spin; only some build snares like this one. She does not watch the corridor. Every strand is a nerve she borrows, and she feels it.

shot.subject: firefly
shot.notes: a lantern weaves between the trunks, its glow brushing the darkness

segments.text: Night. A firefly works its slow signal down the corridor — flash, drift, flash — a courtship light it cannot switch off. That glow is a message meant for a mate, but a message is only sound in the dark: anyone may be listening.

shot.subject: web
shot.notes: the strike: silk snaps taut; the light lurches, tilts, and jerks in place

segments.text: And the corridor ends early. Every struggle rings the web like a bell, and the bell has an owner. She races down her own signal-lines and binds the catch in fresh silk — then does what spiders do: she cannot chew, so she floods the prey with digestive fluid and drinks it back out, the meal dissolved before it is swallowed.

shot.subject: web
shot.notes: her silhouette crosses the net toward the trembling light; the glow dims to nothing

segments.text: We saw this moment from the firefly's side in episode two, and from hers in episode five. Seen whole, it is neither tragedy nor villainy. It is energy changing hands — the grass's light, taken by the beetle, taken by the spider, one link further from the sun.

shot.subject: ladybug
shot.notes: morning: a ladybug whirs off a trunk, armored and unbothered

segments.text: Day shift. The ladybug wears the boldest colors in the garden, and they are not for show. That red-and-black is a warning — aposematism, the honest advertisement of a foul defense. A ladybug that is seized bleeds a bitter, toxic fluid from its joints, and a bird that tries one learns to leave the color alone.

shot.subject: web
shot.notes: the impact: red shell against white net, wing covers pinned mid-open

segments.text: But a warning only works on something that can see, and can learn. The silk does neither. Glue reads no labels. The bright shell that shouts don't eat me to every eye in the garden says nothing at all to a net — and the ladybug's one perfect defense meets the one hunter it was never built to answer.

shot.subject: butterfly
shot.notes: a butterfly's tumbling path carries it straight toward a web — and past it

segments.text: Most prey, though, survive — and their tricks are the real story of this garden. The butterfly's drunken, tumbling flight evolved to foil birds, and it foils webs too: a flier that cannot hold a straight line is very hard for a fixed trap to intercept. Evasion is a defense, and it is everywhere.

shot.subject: web
shot.notes: a glancing strike: one wingtip brushes a strand, tears free, the flier lurches away

segments.text: So are the others. Camouflage hides the still — the moth matched to bark, the caterpillar shaped like a twig. Numbers hide the many — a swarm too large to be meaningfully hunted. Speed saves the quick. Every prey animal in the corridor is running one of these strategies, and the net only collects the ones whose strategy failed today.

shot.subject: web
shot.notes: a gust bellies the net; debris and one struggling captive shake loose in the flex

segments.text: And a subtler hunter than the spider works the same corridor unseen. The parasitoid wasps take prey the web never could — laying their eggs inside living caterpillars and beetles, their larvae consuming the host from within. Silent, specific, and relentless, they are among the greatest controllers of insect numbers anywhere. Not every predator builds a web. Some build inside you.

shot.subject: tree
shot.notes: rise from the web up through the canopy: the whole meadow beyond, teeming

segments.text: Now pull back and count. Energy climbs this garden in steps — sun to plant to grazer to hunter — and at every step, most of it is lost, spent as heat and living. Only a small fraction, roughly a tenth, carries up to the next level. That single fact rules the whole world you have watched: it is why grass is everywhere and grazers are common, and why the hunters at the top — the spider, the bird — are rare. There is only ever enough sunlight left up here for a few.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: fireflies over the grass at dusk, undiminished, uncountable

segments.text: Which is why the predators matter. A spider is a keystone — a brake on the abundance below her. Remove the hunters and the grazers boom, then strip the plants, then starve. The web taxes the traffic so the meadow never eats itself. For every lantern the silk takes, the field lights a hundred more, and the balance holds.

shot.subject: spider
shot.notes: she repairs a torn sector, spinneret to strand, methodical

segments.text: And nothing here is wasted. What the hunters miss, the decomposers claim — the worms, the fungi, the unseen recyclers that break every death back down into soil, feeding it to the grass, returning it to the sun's first passengers. The chain is not a line. It is a loop, and it never spills a drop.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: from the treeline down to the waterline, where the ground dampens and darkens

segments.text: The web is the garden's driest danger. Its wettest is next — a hazard that does not wait in corridors but arrives, on schedule, over the border itself.

shot.subject: fish
shot.notes: a fish rising toward the flooded margin, silhouetted against drowned grass

segments.text: When the waters rise, the map becomes a menu. Next: When the Waters Rise.
